Price test Compliance+ at $790 with quarterly SRA before wide rollout.
Sign 2 practice-consultant referral partners before paid spend.
Build the audit-binder demo (redacted) — the compliance proof IS the demo.
Adopt a written review-response policy (no PHI, ever) before scaling review volume.

intro.h:
Never lose a morning
to a dead server.
intro.s: Managed IT + HIPAA compliance for dental practices — and the plan to grow it across DFW.
It's a schedule full of no-shows, a hygienist paid to wait, and an imaging vendor pointing at your network guy.
9-minute response · audit-ready compliance · one number to call.

v-overview.title: Overview
v-overview.h1: Never lose a morning to a dead server.
v-overview.sub: 12 interlocking docs for a geo-bound B2B service — managed IT for dental practices across Dallas–Fort Worth. The through-line: downtime is measured in empty chairs, and the buyer needs compliance they can show an auditor. (Fictional demo company; all numbers are illustrative.)

v-product.title: Service context
v-product.sub: The shared brief every other doc reads from.

kv: Service
Enamel IT — managed IT, security & HIPAA compliance for dental practices
What it does
Monitoring + helpdesk + imaging-vendor management + a quarterly, evidence-backed compliance binder
Geography
Dallas–Fort Worth metro (onsite within 45 min); no remote-only accounts
Delivery
Per-location retainer · named engineer pod · 9-min median response (trailing 12 mo)
Stage
32 practices under management · expanding from Collin County into Tarrant

blocks.callout: POV. A dental practice is a small hospital wearing a small business's IT budget. The stakes are clinical (imaging, records, schedule) but the buyer has no IT staff — so the service must carry the accountability, not hand back tickets.

blocks.h: Where the money is lost today

v-personas.title: ICP & personas
v-personas.sub: Select a persona to inspect JTBD, pain, and the message that lands.

rows.t: Dr. Reyes — solo owner-dentist primary
rows.d: Keep every chair producing; never think about IT.
One number to call and an audit binder that's always current.
Standardize IT across locations with per-operatory economics.

blocks.callout: Practices that want break-fix hourly billing, DSOs with corporate IT, and anyone outside the 45-minute onsite radius. Retainer economics and the response SLA both break — refer them out instead.

v-positioning.title: Positioning
v-positioning.sub: Statement, category, value themes, and the tagline tester.

blocks.callout: For dental practices in DFW with no IT staff, Enamel IT is the dental-specialized MSP that keeps chairs producing and compliance audit-ready — with a 9-minute median response and one accountable retainer, instead of a generalist queue or a cousin with a login.

kv: Category
Dental-specialized managed IT (vertical MSP) — not "computer support"
Value themes
Never chair-downAudit-readyOne throat to choke
Best-fit market
1–8 location practices, no internal IT, digital imaging in every op
Why now
Imaging is now 100% digital, OCR enforcement is up, and the metro's generalist MSPs quote 4-hour SLAs a practice can't survive

blocks.h: Tagline tester — copy & star your pick

v-messaging.title: Messaging
v-messaging.sub: POV → value prop → three pillars. Select a pillar to inspect proof; switch persona variants with the tabs.

kv: Roof · POV
Downtime is a schedule full of no-shows — not an IT ticket.
Value proposition
Chairs stay producing, compliance stays provable, and there's one number to call.

rows.d: 9-min median response; imaging treated as clinical, not office, IT.
A living binder: SRA, training logs, BAAs, evidence — shown quarterly.
We own the imaging-vendor call so the practice never referees.

v-competitive.title: Competitive
v-competitive.sub: The real incumbent is informal IT. Select an alternative to inspect when-we-win / when-they-win + guidance.

rows.d: Informal IT: a relative, a retired tech, break-fix hourly
Competent, but dental-blind: no imaging expertise, 4-hr SLA
Vertical brand, remote-first delivery, call-center support
Per hour, yes. Per chair-down morning — one rescheduled day costs more than a quarter of the retainer. We publish the math.
Neither had anyone, until the Tuesday they did. The binder isn't fear — it's what you hand the auditor so the letter stays a letter.
They handle their software. When it's the network, the server, or the backup, they hand it back — to whoever answers our line in 9 minutes.

v-reviews.title: Reviews desk
v-reviews.sub: For a local service, Google reviews ARE the funnel. This desk mines ours and every rival's. (Demo fiction — quotes are illustrative, typed and dated per the evidence contract.)

kv: Scope
Google Business profiles: Enamel + 6 named DFW rivals · Yelp (secondary) · trailing 24 mo
Ratings shape
Enamel 4.9★ (61) · generalist rivals cluster 4.2–4.6★ · franchise 3.8★ (support-queue complaints trending down since Jan)

evidence.q: Server died at 7:05, tech was in our Plano office by 7:40, first patient seated on time.
First IT company that talks to Dexis and Eaglesoft support FOR us instead of pointing fingers.

blocks.h: What buyers punish (rivals, 1–2★) — our objection bank

evidence.q: Took two days to get a callback while our x-ray sensors were down. Two DAYS.
Every call starts over with a new person at a call center who has never heard of our practice.

blocks.callout: Switching language to reuse verbatim: "two days for a callback", "starts over with a new person", "pointing fingers at the imaging vendor." These phrases go into ads, the homepage, and the consultant one-pager — buyer words, not ours.

v-pricing.title: Pricing
v-pricing.sub: Value metric = locations + operatories (chairs), because that's how the practice makes money. Compliance is the premium tier, not an add-on menu.

blocks.p: Anchor to the cost of a chair-down morning (a 14-patient reschedule), never to hourly rates — hourly is the incumbent's frame.

v-launch.title: Growth plan
v-launch.sub: Services GTM: referrals + local proof + field presence. No PLG — a practice buys twice a decade, so every touch must compound trust.

head: Phase
Move
rows: GBP rebuild + review cadence · consultant/CPA one-pager · 2 study-club talks
Southwest Dental Conference booth · 3-location case study published · referral program live
Per-city SEO pages · paid search on "dental IT support {city}" · price test Compliance+

v-referral.title: Referral engine
v-referral.sub: The services counterpart to PLG: the client base is the growth channel — 32 practices that talk to each other at study clubs.

flow: Ask: "who else should sleep this well?"
Intro at study club / dental society

list: Practice-consultant loop — consultants + dental CPAs touch dozens of practices; one-pager + rev-share makes Enamel their default answer.
Imaging-vendor loop — being the MSP that Dexis/Eaglesoft techs LIKE working with turns rival tickets into referrals.
Review loop — every resolved emergency triggers a review ask (written policy: never confirm the practice is a client in replies, no PHI).

kv: Both sides
one free month per signed referral
PQL-equivalent
a practice that mentions expansion, an audit letter, or an imaging migration in any QBR

v-events.title: Events desk
v-events.sub: Geo-scoped: DFW + Texas statewide. For a local service, one good booth conversation is worth a quarter of paid search.

head: Event
When
Why / play
rows: the metro's flagship — booth + the chair-down-math handout
statewide credibility; speak on ransomware readiness
the real buying committee — 20-min compliance talks, no pitch
sponsor the CE credit, own the intro

blocks.p: Rule: talks teach (compliance, downtime math) — the booth sells. Never reverse it.

v-channels.title: Channels desk
v-channels.sub: Where a practice actually looks when IT breaks — ranked by trust, not reach.

head: Channel
Role
Note
rows: the engine; everything else feeds it
4.9★ with fresh reviews wins the 2am search
"dental IT support Plano" — low volume, perfect intent
turn on after reviews + pages are strong

v-narrative.title: Narrative
v-narrative.sub: The change → stakes → villain → promised land → proof → flag.

rows.d: Dentistry went fully digital — imaging, records, schedule. The practice became a small hospital on a small-business IT budget.
When it breaks, the cost isn't a ticket — it's a rescheduled morning, a paid-but-idle team, and an auditor's letter.
Accountability gaps: the hourly guy, the 4-hour SLA, the vendor finger-point. Nobody OWNS the outcome.
Chairs full, binder current, one number to call — IT the practice never has to think about.
9-min median response (published, trailing 12 mo), $0 fines across 32 practices, 97% retention.
Enamel IT: the dental-specialized MSP. We keep the chairs producing.

copy: "Downtime is a schedule full of no-shows."
Downtime is a schedule full of no-shows.
"A chair-down morning costs more than a quarter of the retainer."
A chair-down morning costs more than a quarter of the retainer.

v-campaign.title: Campaign
v-campaign.sub: Flagship campaign. Big idea: publish the cost of a chair-down morning and make every rival's SLA answer for it.

kv: Big idea
"Chair-down math" — a calculator + one-pager that prices downtime in patients, not hours. Every channel points at it.
Channels
Consultant/CPA one-pager · GBP + reviews · study-club talks · SWDC booth · per-city SEO · paid search (phase 2)
Deliverables
Calculator page · one-pager · case study · 3 talk decks · booth kit · review-response policy · 6 city pages
Guardrails
No fear-mongering ads, no PHI ever in review replies, no naming rivals — the math does the arguing.

blocks.callout: Verdict: greenlight — the vertical focus and the published SLA are real differentiation. Do not scale paid spend until two consultant partnerships are signed; for a trust-bought service, paid without referral proof just buys expensive skepticism.

kv: JTBD
Keep every chair producing; never personally think about IT.
Pain
Lost a Monday morning to a dead imaging server; the hourly guy was unreachable.
Buying
Trusts peers + consultants; decides in the QBR, not the pitch.

copy: "A chair-down morning costs you 14 patients. We answer in 9 minutes."
A chair-down morning costs you 14 patients. We answer in 9 minutes — median, not marketing.

kv: JTBD
One number to call; an audit binder that's always current.
Pain
Referees fights between the imaging vendor and whoever set up the network.
Power
Doesn't sign, but a veto in practice — win Monica first.

copy: "One number, one invoice, and the binder is always audit-ready."
One number to call, one invoice, and the audit binder is always current.

kv: JTBD
Standardize IT across 3–8 locations with predictable per-operatory cost.
Pain
Each location inherited a different IT setup and a different guy.
Angle
per-operatory pricing + a single pod that knows every site.

copy: "Three locations, one IT bill that scales per operatory."
Three locations, one IT bill that scales per operatory — not per emergency.

blocks.p: Response measured and published: 9-minute median, trailing 12 months. Imaging is treated as clinical infrastructure — monitored, spared, and drilled like it.
Compliance as a living binder, not an annual scramble: security risk assessment, training logs, BAAs, and incident evidence — reviewed with the practice every quarter.

list: The binder demo closes deals — it's the product made visible

blocks.p: Enamel owns the imaging-vendor call, the ISP call, and the printer nobody admits to buying. The practice never referees a finger-pointing match again.

list: Vendor escalations handled under Enamel's ticket, not the practice's phone

blocks.p: Any practice that has already lost a morning; anyone facing an audit letter; group practices (one person can't cover 3 sites).
Genuinely tiny practices that see IT as a cost to minimize — often fine until the Tuesday they aren't. Don't chase; stay referable.
Never mock the nephew — he's family. Price the downtime, not the person: "per hour he's cheaper; per chair-down morning he isn't."
Imaging expertise (Dexis/Eaglesoft/Open Dental fluency), the 9-min vs 4-hr SLA, and the compliance binder they don't offer.
The practice shares an owner with a non-dental business and wants one vendor for both.
Ask one question: "who does your current MSP call when the sensor driver breaks the bridge to the practice-management system?" Silence is the demo.
Local: onsite in 45 minutes vs a remote queue; a named pod vs a call center; reviews that say so (see the Reviews desk).
Multi-state DSOs standardizing nationally — out of ICP anyway.
Quote their own public reviews' language back — "starts over with a new person every call" — and put the named-pod org chart on the table.
